The differential equation models the growth of the beetle population, incorporating both the logistic growth and the seasonal variation due to rainfall. The general solution for $ P(t) $ can be found by solving the nonhomogeneous linear differential equation:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = rP \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right)P
$$

This equation is a combination of a logistic growth term and a sinusoidal perturbation. The solution involves finding the homogeneous solution and a particular solution. The homogeneous solution satisfies:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = rP \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right)
$$

which has the general solution:

$$
P_h(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\frac{K}{P_0} - 1\right)e^{-rt}}
$$

The particular solution can be found using the method of undetermined coefficients or by recognizing the sinusoidal term as a forcing function. The full solution is the sum of the homogeneous and particular solutions. The particular solution will involve a sinusoidal function with amplitude $ A $ and frequency $ \frac{2\pi}{12} $, which corresponds to a seasonal cycle of 12 months.

The maximum population occurs when the derivative $ \frac{dP}{dt} $ is zero. This happens when the logistic growth term balances the sinusoidal term. The maximum population is expected to occur when the sinusoidal term is at its peak, which occurs at $ t = 6 $ months (midpoint of the year). The sinusoidal term influences the timing of the peaks by shifting them slightly forward or backward depending on the phase of the sine wave.

The enthusiast should watch for the peaks around $ t = 6 $ months, but the sinusoidal term may cause the peaks to shift slightly. This means the timing of the beetle-watching activities may need to be adjusted to account for the seasonal variations in the population. The impact of the sinusoidal term is that it introduces a periodic variation in the population, which can affect the timing and intensity of the peaks. The enthusiast should be aware of these variations to optimize their beetle-watching activities.
